bibliographic_data.title: Control in the Presence of Manipulators: Cooperative and Competitive Cases
bibliographic_data.abstract: Control and manipulation are two of the most studied types of attacks on elections. In this paper, we study the complexity of control attacks on elections in which there are manipulators. We study both the case where the “chair” who is seeking to control the election is allied with the manipulators, and the case where the manipulators seek to thwart the chair. In the latter case, we see that the order of play substantially influences the complexity. We prove upper bounds, holding over every election system with a polynomial-time winner problem, for all standard control cases, and some of these bounds are at the second or third level of the polynomial hierarchy, and we provide matching lower bounds to prove these tight. Nonetheless, for important natural systems the complexity can be much lower. We prove that for approval and plurality elections, the complexity of even competitive clashes between a controller and manipulators falls far below those high bounds, even as low as polynomial time. Yet for a Borda-voting case we show that such clashes raise the complexity unless NP = coNP.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper studies control attacks on elections in the presence of manipulators, providing upper and lower bounds for various election systems, including showing that approval and plurality elections can have polynomial-time complexity even in competitive clashes.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors define natural problems to study the computational complexity of control under cooperative and competitive scenarios with manipulators. They prove upper and lower bounds using existing hardness results and novel approaches.
outcomes: Upper bounds for all standard control cases are proven, some at high levels of the polynomial hierarchy.
Matching lower bounds are provided, proving tightness of these upper bounds.
For approval and plurality elections, competitive clashes between a controller and manipulators have complexity as low as polynomial time.
For Borda voting, competitive clashes raise the complexity unless NP = coNP.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Elections are an important tool in decision-making. This paper investigates control attacks on elections where there are manipulators. The authors define cooperative and competitive scenarios involving a 'chair' who seeks to control the election with or against manipulative voters, respectively. They prove upper bounds for various election systems, some of which reach high levels of the polynomial hierarchy. However, they also show that approval and plurality elections can have lower complexity in competitive scenarios, even as low as polynomial time. For Borda voting, the complexity increases unless NP = coNP.
